insights: Fred Luddy's resolution time degraded from 87 hours in Q1 2023 (better than peers' 114 hours) to 410 hours in Q3–Q4 2023 — a 10x slowdown relative to other agents by Q4 — indicating a sharp, time-bound operational breakdown rather than chronic underperformance.

Fred Luddy's performance dip is uniform across all incident priorities (Critical and High) and categories (Network and Software), with resolution times consistently 3–4x higher than peers in every segment, ruling out workload composition or incident complexity as explanatory factors.

Fred Luddy has 7 incidents still 'In Progress' — more than all other agents combined — and his maximum resolution time (1370 hours) is 3–4x higher than any peer's (305–405 hours), signaling acute backlog accumulation and potential service risk escalation.

Fred Luddy's closure rate dropped to 75% in Q4 2023 versus 96% for other agents, coinciding with his resolution time deterioration, confirming a systemic decline in end-to-end incident ownership and timely resolution capability.

The schedule field — required to assess absence correlation — is 83% null overall and only covers Fred Luddy for June 2023 (Q2), predating his Q3–Q4 performance collapse; therefore, absence-driven workload redistribution cannot be validated or ruled out with current data.

Fred Luddy’s incident volume remained stable (16–24 incidents per quarter) throughout the degradation period, confirming that the performance collapse is not attributable to increased workload volume but likely reflects internal capacity constraints or process-level failure.
summary: Fred Luddy’s performance collapsed sharply starting Q3 2023 — shifting from best-in-class resolution time (87 hours) to worst-in-class (410+ hours) and closure rate (75% in Q4) — with no change in incident volume, priority mix, or category distribution, confirming a non-workload-related operational failure. The degradation is uniform across all incident types and severity levels, and manifests concretely in severe backlog (7 'In Progress' incidents) and extreme outliers in resolution duration (1370 hours). Critically, absence analysis — a key hypothesized cause — is blocked: the schedule field is 83% null and contains no usable data for the Q3–Q4 degradation window. Given the evidence, the root cause lies outside observable workload drivers and requires investigation into agent-specific operational context (e.g., system access, tooling, supervision changes, or unrecorded leave) — not staffing or assignment patterns. Next focus should be on qualitative review of Fred Luddy’s Q3–Q4 support interactions, system logs, or manager escalation records.
